mnemonics: Chapman-Kolmogorov for n Steps: 'Multiply Matrices, Not Probabilities.' This reminds you that to find the probability of going from state i to state j in n steps, you must raise the one-step transition matrix (P) to the nth power (P^n) and look at the (i, j)th entry, rather than trying to multiply individual probabilities incorrectly.
keyPoints: The (i, j)th entry of the matrix P^n (P raised to the power n) gives the n-step transition probability from state i to state j.
The Chapman-Kolmogorov equations provide a method to compute these probabilities by decomposing the path into shorter segments.
The long-term behavior of a Markov chain is analyzed by examining the limits of these higher transition probabilities as n approaches infinity.
commonMistakes: Assuming P(n) = n * P(1): Incorrectly thinking the n-step probability is just the one-step probability multiplied by n.
Confusing matrix exponentiation (P^n) with element-wise exponentiation: Raising each element of the matrix to the power n instead of performing matrix multiplication.
Forgetting that the Chapman-Kolmogorov equations require summing over all possible intermediate states k at time m.
